author: @micksayers4528
text:
Hi Robert, you just managed to demonstrate the work i have been investigating on how to power an LED for your competition. I ordered 18650 Li-ion batteries which haven't arrived yet but should before the competition closing date. there are obviously some slight changes with electronic features and current supply to the board. would have loved to use LiFePO4 cells but they were twice the price, would make the electronic design, because of the similar nominal voltage to that of an LED, a lot more simple and safe with more than 10 times charge discharge cycles than Li-ion, a lot safer and cleaner technology. Also the LiFePO4 batteries would have arrived after the close of the competition.
Never mind.
Will still make a video of my set up.
Mick

author: @Earzone63
text: Awesome conversion! One thing I have to ask about is those ~£15 transformers/inverters inside the units that recharge nickel cadmium cells, I wondered how someone could make plug-in stuff more portable, without needing a ridiculously oversized inverter that costing >£100, (i.e reversing the flow of current from the batteries into a female to female three pin plug… Maybe the available current draw would be far too low however.)

author: @TheClembo
text: Love the channel BUT do wish you would give web-links to the gear you use in the video. I spend twice as long looking for products as I do watching you! -thank you.

author: @nicgreen4234
text: Great video on so many levels Rob, thanks. When paired with the cheap charging circuits you show it gives years of life to old phone cells, and say goodbye to disposable batteries. One problem I've come across is small led torches (flashlights)  relying on the small current available from disposable AAA cells don't like the high current from the li-ion cells. As in killed instantly! Led's are largely voltage tolerant but current specific. The opposite of filament globes!

author: @themadscotsman2159
text: I discovered these lithium battery charging boards about a year ago and one project was to add it to a solar powered PIR security light to charge the battery fully when it receives insufficient sunlight in winter for a full charge, it works a treat. I've recently been scavenging batteries when I get the opportunity in order to repurpose them. A trip to my local phone repair shop is on my to do list and beg for some before they hit the bin. Thanks for a great video, I have some new ideas for projects now.

author: @uksuperrascal
text: Robert - For a more versatile PCB try the Micro USB 3.7V Lithium Li-ion 18650 Battery Charger Module Boost Step Up Variable 5V to 12V This will charge from any phone charger and can be set to run 9V  PP3 Equipment.

text: a lot of those lights on a string that are LED these days have a box that takes like 3 AAA batteries ie perfect for liPo cells actually since they work down I think to like 3 or 2.5 volt)

replies.author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
replies.text: oh also as most things that use standard batteries, also are designed to allow using rechargable like cadmium sulfide, metal hydroxide ect, which means the voltage is 3 x 1.2v not 3 x 1.5 only, so 3.6 is perfect for those, it seems.

author: @Zodliness
text: At 04:53 One must observe the difference between series and parallel lithium battery connections, Because you seem to have edited (chopped) that little nugget out of the original upload. Balancing chargers are always a crucial addition, if you're wiring two or more lithium cells in series.

text: Good demo Rob. What's nice about those circuit boards is that they usually also switch the output off if the voltage drops below a certain point. That point is usually a bit low, but is good enough to stop the cells from being damaged.

author: @john_blues
text: What happens if the batteries don't have the same capacitance?

replies.author: @j.f.christ8421
replies.text: You'll get current flowing from the "bigger" one to the smaller one.  This isn't a good thing as you're wasting power and may cause overheating, so you try to use matching ones.  Also even if you do have 2 matching ones, make sure the have the same amount of charge when you solder them together.  It's a bit safer if you discharge them first.

replies.author: @j.f.christ8421

text: Also don't forget to cover the terminals with kapton tape to keep it from shorting out against anything conductive it may touch.

author: @TrentTationnaiseXization
text:
I have a strip of them charging boards I was gunna use for a mass 18650 charger but I think I prefer this idea, I also got a bucket of lithium batteries from laptops to go with.

A voltage regulator at the output could widen the voltage range to play with.

replies.author: @TrentTationnaiseXization
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Robert words cant describe how awesome you are.

replies.author: @j.f.christ8421
replies.text: If you have a look at the datasheet for those little boards, you can change a single resistor to set the charging current to anywhere up to an amp, IIRC they come set at 500mA.  From memory it's up to a 20k resistor, I have one with a pot on it so I can easily change the charge rate.  The chip also supports a thermal cut-out which I've never tried.
